questions.title: What does print() do?
questions.feedback: print() displays its argument on the screen. It is the most basic way to produce output in Python.

questions.title: Which line prints Python?
questions.feedback: In Python, print() is the function used to show output. Always wrap the text in quotes.

questions.title: Fill in the blank
questions.feedback: The function is called print. You call it with parentheses and pass the text inside quotes.

questions.title: Trace the output
questions.feedback: Each print() call starts on a new line. Two print calls produce two lines of output.

questions.title: What is a comment?
questions.prompt: What is a comment in Python?

choices.text: A note that Python ignores when running

questions.feedback: Comments are notes for humans — Python skips them when running your code.

questions.title: Comment symbol
questions.prompt: Which line is a Python comment?
questions.feedback: Python comments start with the # symbol. Everything after # on that line is ignored.

questions.title: Fill in the comment
questions.prompt: A Python comment starts with: `___ This is ignored`
questions.feedback: The # symbol marks the start of a comment in Python.

questions.title: What runs?
questions.feedback: The comment # Say hello is ignored. Only print('Hi!') runs, so the output is Hi!

questions.title: What is a variable?
questions.prompt: What is a variable in Python?

choices.text: A named box that stores a value

questions.feedback: A variable is like a labeled box. You give it a name, and it holds a value you can use later.

questions.title: Assigning a value
questions.prompt: Which line correctly stores the number 10 in a variable named `score`?
questions.feedback: Use a single = to assign a value. The variable name comes first, then = then the value.

questions.title: Trace the output
questions.feedback: print(age) prints the value stored in age, which is 25. Without quotes, Python looks up the variable.

questions.title: Fill the assignment
questions.prompt: Complete: `name ___ 'Alice'` to store the string in name
questions.feedback: A single = is the assignment operator. It stores the right-hand value into the left-hand variable.

questions.title: Valid name?
questions.prompt: Which is a valid Python variable name?
questions.feedback: Variable names cannot have spaces or start with a number. Use underscores instead of spaces.

questions.title: Updating a variable
questions.feedback: x starts at 3, then x = x + 2 adds 2 to make 5. The new value is stored back in x.

questions.title: Shorthand update
questions.feedback: x += 4 is shorthand for x = x + 4. Since x is 6, the result is 10.

questions.title: Fill the shorthand
questions.feedback: x -= 1 is shorthand for x = x - 1. It subtracts 1 from x and stores the result.

questions.title: What is a string?
questions.prompt: What is a string in Python?
questions.feedback: A string is text data — a sequence of characters enclosed in quotes.

questions.title: String length
questions.feedback: len() counts the number of characters. 'code' has 4 characters: c, o, d, e.

questions.title: String concatenation
questions.feedback: The + operator joins (concatenates) strings. The space is part of ' World' so the result is 'Hello World'.

questions.title: Repeat a string
questions.feedback: Multiplying a string by a number repeats it. 'ha' * 3 gives 'hahaha'.

questions.title: Upper case
questions.feedback: .upper() converts every character to uppercase. 'hello' becomes 'HELLO'.

questions.title: Strip whitespace
questions.feedback: .strip() removes leading and trailing whitespace. '  hi  ' becomes 'hi'.

questions.title: Lower case
questions.feedback: .lower() converts all characters to lowercase. 'Python' becomes 'python'.

questions.title: Match string methods
questions.prompt: Match each string method to what it does.
questions.feedback: String methods let you transform text without changing the original variable.

questions.title: What is an integer?
questions.prompt: Which of these is an integer (whole number)?
questions.feedback: Integers are whole numbers with no decimal point. 42 is an int; 3.14 is a float; '42' is a string.

questions.title: Division result
questions.feedback: In Python 3, / always gives a float result. 7 / 2 is 3.5, not 3.

questions.title: Floor division
questions.feedback: // is floor division — it divides and rounds down to the nearest whole number. 7 // 2 is 3.

questions.title: Modulo
questions.feedback: % is the modulo operator — it gives the remainder. 10 divided by 3 is 3 remainder 1.

questions.title: Exponent
questions.feedback: ** is the power operator. 2 ** 3 means 2 to the power of 3, which is 2×2×2 = 8.

questions.title: Order of operations
questions.feedback: Python follows standard math order: multiplication before addition. 3 * 4 = 12, then 2 + 12 = 14.

questions.title: Match operators
questions.prompt: Match each operator to its meaning.
questions.feedback: Python has many math operators. Learning them all gives you powerful tools for calculations.

questions.title: What is a Boolean?
questions.prompt: What type of value is `True` in Python?
questions.feedback: True and False are Boolean values. They represent yes/no or on/off states.

questions.title: Comparison result
questions.feedback: 5 is greater than 3, so 5 > 3 evaluates to True.

questions.title: Equality check
questions.feedback: == checks if two values are equal. Since 3 equals 3, the result is True.

questions.title: not operator
questions.feedback: The `not` operator flips a Boolean. not True gives False.

questions.title: and operator
questions.feedback: `and` requires BOTH sides to be True. If either is False, the result is False.

questions.title: or operator
questions.feedback: `or` only needs ONE side to be True. False or True is True.

questions.title: Match logic operators
questions.prompt: Match each logical operator to its rule.

matchingPairs.right: At least one must be True

questions.feedback: Logical operators combine or invert Boolean values. Master these to write powerful conditions.

questions.title: What keyword starts a condition?
questions.prompt: Which keyword starts a conditional statement in Python?
questions.feedback: The `if` keyword starts a conditional block. If the condition is True, the indented code runs.

questions.title: Trace the if
questions.feedback: x is 10, which is greater than 5, so the condition is True and 'Big' is printed.

questions.title: if-else
questions.feedback: 40 is not >= 50, so the if-block is skipped and the else-block runs, printing 'Fail'.

questions.title: Arrange an if-else
questions.prompt: Arrange these lines to print 'cold' when temp < 15, else 'warm'.
questions.feedback: The if-block runs when the condition is True; the else-block runs otherwise.
questions.rawMarkdown:
Blocks:
```python
temp = 10
if temp < 15:
    print('cold')
else:
    print('warm')
```
*Correct order:*
```python
temp = 10
if temp < 15:
    print('cold')
else:
    print('warm')
```

questions.title: elif meaning
questions.feedback: elif is short for 'else if'. It adds more conditions to check when the first `if` is False.

questions.title: Trace elif
questions.feedback: 75 is not >= 90, but 75 >= 70 is True, so the elif branch runs and prints 'B'.

questions.title: Write a classifier
questions.prompt: Write a function `classify(n)` that returns 'positive' if n > 0, 'negative' if n < 0, or 'zero' if n == 0.
questions.feedback: Use if/elif/else to check all three cases. Return the string that matches the condition.

questions.title: List syntax
questions.prompt: Which correctly creates a list of three numbers?
questions.feedback: Lists use square brackets []. (1,2,3) is a tuple and {1,2,3} is a set.

questions.title: List indexing
questions.feedback: Python lists are zero-indexed. Index 0 is the first item, so fruits[0] is 'apple'.

questions.title: Append to list
questions.prompt: What does `.append()` do to a list?

choices.text: Adds an item to the end

questions.feedback: .append() adds one item to the end of the list. It modifies the list in place.

questions.title: Trace list operation
questions.feedback: The list starts with 3 items, then .append(40) adds one more. len() returns 4.

questions.title: For loop keyword
questions.prompt: Which keyword starts a loop that iterates over items?
questions.feedback: The `for` keyword is used to loop. It goes through each item in a sequence one at a time.

questions.title: Trace range loop
questions.prompt: How many times does this loop run?
questions.feedback: range(4) produces 0, 1, 2, 3 — four values. The loop runs four times.

questions.title: Loop over list
questions.feedback: The for loop prints each item on its own line: first 'red', then 'green'.

questions.title: Arrange a loop
questions.prompt: Arrange these lines to sum numbers 1 through 3.
questions.code:
total = 0
for n in [1, 2, 3]:
    total += n
print(total)
questions.feedback: First initialize total, then loop and add each number, then print the final sum.
questions.rawMarkdown:
Blocks:
```python
total = 0
for n in [1, 2, 3]:
    total += n
print(total)
```
*Correct order:*
```python
total = 0
for n in [1, 2, 3]:
    total += n
print(total)
```

questions.title: While loop condition
questions.prompt: A while loop runs as long as its condition is…
questions.feedback: A while loop keeps running as long as its condition evaluates to True.

questions.title: Trace while
questions.feedback: i starts at 0 and increments. When i reaches 3, the condition i < 3 is False and the loop stops.

questions.title: break statement
questions.prompt: What does `break` do inside a loop?
questions.feedback: `break` immediately exits the loop, skipping any remaining iterations.

questions.title: What keyword defines a function?
questions.prompt: Which keyword do you use to define a function in Python?
questions.feedback: In Python, `def` is used to define a function. It stands for 'define'.

questions.title: Trace a function call
questions.feedback: Defining a function does not run it. Calling greet() runs the code inside and prints 'Hi!'.

questions.title: Fill the def
questions.prompt: Complete: `___ say_hello():` to define a function named say_hello
questions.feedback: Every function definition starts with the `def` keyword followed by the function name and parentheses.

questions.title: What is a parameter?
questions.prompt: In `def add(a, b):`, what are `a` and `b`?
questions.feedback: Parameters are the inputs a function accepts. They are listed inside the parentheses in the def line.

questions.title: Trace return
questions.feedback: add(3, 4) calls the function with a=3 and b=4. It returns 3+4=7, which is then printed.

questions.title: Fill the return
questions.prompt: Complete: `___ a * b` to send back the product
questions.feedback: The `return` keyword sends a value back to wherever the function was called from.

questions.title: Write a square function
questions.prompt: Write a function `square(n)` that returns n multiplied by itself.
questions.feedback: return n * n (or n ** 2) gives you n squared.

questions.title: Function with loop
questions.prompt: Write a function `sum_list(nums)` that returns the sum of all numbers in the list.
questions.feedback: Use a for loop and a running total variable. Start total at 0 and add each item.

questions.title: Find max in list
questions.prompt: Write a function `find_max(nums)` that returns the largest number in the list. Assume the list is non-empty.
questions.feedback: Start with best = nums[0], then loop through the rest and update best when you find a larger value.

placementQuiz.title: Basic Output
placementQuiz.prompt: Which line prints the word Hello to the screen?
placementQuiz.feedback: In Python, print() is used to display output.

placementQuiz.title: Variable Assignment
placementQuiz.prompt: Which line correctly stores 42 in a variable named `answer`?
placementQuiz.feedback: Use a single = to assign. Variable name goes on the left.

placementQuiz.title: String Concatenation
placementQuiz.feedback: The + operator joins strings together. 'Py' + 'thon' gives 'Python'.

placementQuiz.title: List Indexing
placementQuiz.feedback: Lists are zero-indexed. Index 1 is the second item, which is 'b'.

placementQuiz.title: Boolean Result
placementQuiz.feedback: 10 is greater than 5, so the comparison evaluates to True.

placementQuiz.title: For Loop Range
placementQuiz.prompt: How many times does `for i in range(3):` loop?
placementQuiz.feedback: range(3) produces 0, 1, 2 — exactly 3 values, so the loop runs 3 times.

placementQuiz.title: Function Definition
placementQuiz.prompt: Which line correctly defines a function named `greet`?
placementQuiz.feedback: Python uses the `def` keyword to define functions.

placementQuiz.title: Return Value
placementQuiz.feedback: double(5) calls the function with x=5. It returns 5*2=10, which print() displays.
